From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Nontrinitarian sect of Christianity

This article is about the Christian theological tradition. For the doctrinal position, see [Nontrinitarianism](/wiki/Nontrinitarianism "Nontrinitarianism"). For the liberal religious movement, see [Unitarian Universalism](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism"). For its British counterpart, see [General Assembly of Unitarian and Free Christian Churches](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_Unitarian_and_Free_Christian_Churches "General Assembly of Unitarian and Free Christian Churches"). For other uses, see [Unitarian](/wiki/Unitarian_(disambiguation) "Unitarian (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Theodore_Parker_statue.JPG/250px-Theodore_Parker_statue.JPG)](/wiki/File:Theodore_Parker_statue.JPG)

Unitarian minister [Theodore Parker](/wiki/Theodore_Parker "Theodore Parker") (1810–1860) was a prominent reformer and abolitionist. His statue is in front of the [Theodore Parker Unitarian Universalist Church](/wiki/Theodore_Parker_Unitarian_Universalist_Church "Theodore Parker Unitarian Universalist Church") in West Roxbury, Massachusetts.

**Unitarianism**[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a [nontrinitarian](/wiki/Nontrinitarianism "Nontrinitarianism") [movement of Christianity](/wiki/List_of_Christian_movements "List of Christian movements"), which affirms the [unitary](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/unitary "wikt:unitary") [nature of God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") as the singular and unique [creator of the universe](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity").[[2]](#cite_note-Bremer_2015-2) Unitarian theology critiques the [traditional Christian theology](/wiki/History_of_Christian_theology "History of Christian theology") of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity"), which regarded [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") as three distinct but unified beings—transcendent [Creator God](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"), human Savior God (i.e., [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ")), and immanent Spiritual God (i.e., the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity")). Unitarians viewed this understanding of God as a later theological corruption, and they embraced a view of God as a singular, unified entity; in most Unitarian theological interpretations, [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") retains highest respect as a spiritual and moral teacher of unparalleled insight and sensitivity, but [he is not regarded as divine](/wiki/Christology "Christology"), or at least his divine nature is not on the same level as the singular and unique [Creator God](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity").[[2]](#cite_note-Bremer_2015-2)

While many Unitarians believe that [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") was [inspired by God](/wiki/Divine_inspiration "Divine inspiration") in his moral teachings and that he is the [savior](/wiki/Redeemer_(Christianity) "Redeemer (Christianity)") of mankind,[[2]](#cite_note-Bremer_2015-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Miano_2003_15-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) but he is not equal to God himself. Accordingly, Unitarians reject the [Ecumenical Councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_Councils "Ecumenical Councils") and [ecumenical creeds](/wiki/Ecumenical_creeds "Ecumenical creeds"), and sit outside traditional, mainstream Christianity.[[2]](#cite_note-Bremer_2015-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Miano_2003_15-3)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Unitarianism was established in order to restore "[primitive Christianity](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism") before later corruptions set in".[[6]](#cite_note-6) Likewise, Unitarian Christians generally reject the doctrine of [original sin](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The churchmanship of Unitarianism may include [liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity") [denominations](/wiki/Christian_denominations "Christian denominations") or Unitarian Christian denominations that are more [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism"), with the latter being known as [biblical Unitarians](/wiki/Biblical_unitarianism "Biblical unitarianism").[[9]](#cite_note-Larsen2011-9)[[10]](#cite_note-MandelbroteLedger-Lomas2013-10)

The birth of the Unitarian faith is proximate to the [Radical Reformation](/wiki/Radical_Reformation "Radical Reformation"), beginning almost simultaneously among the Protestant[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Polish Brethren](/wiki/Polish_Brethren "Polish Brethren") in the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") and in the [Principality of Transylvania](/wiki/Principality_of_Transylvania_(1570%E2%80%931711) "Principality of Transylvania (1570–1711)") in the mid-16th century;[[12]](#cite_note-Williams_1995-12) the first Unitarian Christian denomination known to have emerged during that time was the [Unitarian Church of Transylvania](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_of_Transylvania "Unitarian Church of Transylvania"), founded by the Unitarian preacher and theologian [Ferenc Dávid](/wiki/Ferenc_D%C3%A1vid "Ferenc Dávid") (c. 1520 – 1579).[[12]](#cite_note-Williams_1995-12) Among its adherents were a significant number of [Italians](/wiki/Italians "Italians") who took refuge in [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"), [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia"), Poland, and Transylvania in order to escape from the [religious persecution](/wiki/Religious_persecution "Religious persecution") perpetrated against them by the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and [Magisterial](/wiki/Magisterial_Reformation "Magisterial Reformation") [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") churches.[[12]](#cite_note-Williams_1995-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Luszczynska_2018-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) In the 17th century, significant repression in Poland led many Unitarians to flee or be killed for their faith.[[13]](#cite_note-Luszczynska_2018-13) From the 16th to 18th centuries, Unitarians in Britain often faced significant political persecution, including [John Biddle](/wiki/John_Biddle_(Unitarian) "John Biddle (Unitarian)") and [Theophilus Lindsey](/wiki/Theophilus_Lindsey "Theophilus Lindsey"). In England, the [first Unitarian Church](/wiki/Essex_Street_Chapel "Essex Street Chapel") was established in 1774 on [Essex Street, London](/wiki/Essex_Street,_London "Essex Street, London"),[[16]](#cite_note-:0-16) where today's [British Unitarian](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_Unitarian_and_Free_Christian_Churches "General Assembly of Unitarian and Free Christian Churches") headquarters is still located.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

As is typical of [dissenters](/wiki/Dissenter "Dissenter") and [nonconformists](/wiki/Nonconformist_Relief_Act_1779 "Nonconformist Relief Act 1779"), Unitarianism does not constitute one single [Christian denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination"); rather, it refers to a collection of both existing and extinct Christian groups (whether historically related to each other or not) that share a common theological concept of the unitary nature of God. Unitarian Christian communities and churches have developed in [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") (mostly [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary")), [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). In [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America"), different schools of Unitarian theology first spread in the [New England Colonies](/wiki/New_England_Colonies "New England Colonies") and subsequently in the [Mid-Atlantic States](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_(United_States) "Mid-Atlantic (United States)"). The first official acceptance of the Unitarian faith on the part of a congregation in North America was by [King's Chapel](/wiki/King%27s_Chapel "King's Chapel") in Boston, from where [James Freeman](/wiki/James_Freeman_(clergyman) "James Freeman (clergyman)") began teaching Unitarian doctrine in 1784 and was appointed rector. Later in 1785, he created a [revised Unitarian *Book of Common Prayer*](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(Unitarian) "Book of Common Prayer (Unitarian)") based on Lindsey's work.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

Unitarianism is a [proper noun](/wiki/Proper_noun "Proper noun") and follows the same English usage as other Christian theologies that have developed within a religious group or denomination (such as [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"), [Anabaptism](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism"), [Adventism](/wiki/Adventism "Adventism"), [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Wesleyanism](/wiki/Wesleyanism "Wesleyanism"), etc.).[[19]](#cite_note-19) The term existed shortly before it became the name of a distinct religious tradition, thus occasionally it is used as a common noun to describe any [understanding of Jesus Christ](/wiki/Christology "Christology") that denies the doctrine of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") or affirms the belief that [God is only one person](/wiki/Monotheism "Monotheism"). In that case, it would be a [Nontrinitarian](/wiki/Nontrinitarianism "Nontrinitarianism") belief system not necessarily associated with the Unitarian movement.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) For example, the Unitarian movement has never accepted the Godhood of Jesus, and therefore does not include those *nontrinitarian* belief systems that do, such as [Oneness Pentecostalism](/wiki/Oneness_Pentecostalism "Oneness Pentecostalism"), [United Pentecostal Church International](/wiki/United_Pentecostal_Church_International "United Pentecostal Church International"), the [True Jesus Church](/wiki/True_Jesus_Church "True Jesus Church"), and the writings of [Michael Servetus](/wiki/Michael_Servetus "Michael Servetus") (all of which maintain that Jesus is God as a single person). Recently, some religious groups have adopted the 19th-century term *[biblical unitarianism](/wiki/Biblical_Unitarianism "Biblical Unitarianism")* to distinguish their theologies from Unitarianism.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Unitarianism is a Christian theology and practice that precedes and is distinct from [Unitarian Universalism](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism").[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) In the 1890s the [American Unitarian Association](/wiki/American_Unitarian_Association "American Unitarian Association") began to allow non-Christian and [non-theistic](/wiki/Non-theism "Non-theism") churches and individuals to be part of their fellowship.[[28]](#cite_note-28) As a result, people who held no Unitarian belief began to be called *Unitarians* because they were members of churches that belonged to the American Unitarian Association. After several decades, the non-theistic members outnumbered the theological Unitarians.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[Ferenc Dávid](/wiki/Ferenc_D%C3%A1vid "Ferenc Dávid") holding his speech at the Diet of Torda (1568), in the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary") (today [Turda](/wiki/Turda "Turda"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania")). Painting by [Aladár Körösfői-Kriesch](/wiki/Alad%C3%A1r_K%C3%B6r%C3%B6sf%C5%91i-Kriesch "Aladár Körösfői-Kriesch") (1896).

Unitarianism, both as a theology and as a [denominational family of churches](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination"), was defined and developed in Poland, Transylvania, England, Wales, India, Japan, Jamaica, the United States, and beyond in the 16th century through the present.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) Although common beliefs existed among Unitarians in each of these regions, they initially grew independently from each other. Only later did they influence one another and accumulate more similarities.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The *Ecclesia minor* or *Minor Reformed Church of Poland*, better known today as the [Polish Brethren](/wiki/Polish_Brethren "Polish Brethren"), was born as the result of a controversy that started on January 22, 1556, when [Piotr of Goniądz](/wiki/Piotr_of_Goni%C4%85dz "Piotr of Goniądz") (Peter Gonesius), a Polish student, spoke out against the [doctrine of the Trinity](/wiki/Doctrine_of_the_Trinity "Doctrine of the Trinity") during the general synod of the Reformed ([Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist")) churches of Poland held in the village of [Secemin](/wiki/Secemin "Secemin").[[33]](#cite_note-33) After nine years of debate, in 1565, the anti-Trinitarians were excluded from the existing synod of the [Polish Reformed Church](/wiki/Polish_Reformed_Church "Polish Reformed Church") (henceforth the *Ecclesia maior*) and they began to hold their own synods as the *Ecclesia minor*. Though frequently called "[Arians](/wiki/Arians "Arians")" by those on the outside, the views of [Fausto Sozzini](/wiki/Fausto_Sozzini "Fausto Sozzini") (Faustus Socinus) became the standard in the church, and these doctrines were quite removed from Arianism. So important was Socinus to the formulation of their beliefs that those outside Poland usually referred to them as [Socinians](/wiki/Socinianism "Socinianism").[[34]](#cite_note-34) The Polish Brethren were disbanded in 1658 by the [Sejm](/wiki/Sejm "Sejm") (Polish Parliament). They were ordered to convert to Roman Catholicism or leave Poland. Most of them went to Transylvania or Holland, where they embraced the name "Unitarian". Between 1665 and 1668 a grandson of Socinus, [Andrzej Wiszowaty Sr.](/wiki/Andrzej_Wiszowaty_Sr. "Andrzej Wiszowaty Sr."), published *[Bibliotheca Fratrum Polonorum](/wiki/Bibliotheca_Fratrum_Polonorum "Bibliotheca Fratrum Polonorum") quos Unitarios vocant* (*Library of the Polish Brethren who are called Unitarians* 4 vols. 1665–1669).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Unitarian Church of Transylvania](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_of_Transylvania "Unitarian Church of Transylvania") was first recognized by the [Edict of Torda](/wiki/Edict_of_Torda "Edict of Torda"), issued by the [Transylvanian Diet](/wiki/Transylvanian_Diet "Transylvanian Diet") under [Prince](/wiki/Rulers_of_Transylvania "Rulers of Transylvania") [John II Sigismund Zápolya](/wiki/John_II_Sigismund_Z%C3%A1polya "John II Sigismund Zápolya") (January 1568),[[35]](#cite_note-poperamet-35) and was first led by [Ferenc Dávid](/wiki/Ferenc_D%C3%A1vid "Ferenc Dávid") (a former [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") bishop, who had begun preaching the new doctrine in 1566). The term "Unitarian" first appeared as *unitaria religio* in a document of the Diet of [Lécfalva](/wiki/Le%C8%9B "Leț"), [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), on 25 October 1600, though it was not widely used in Transylvania until 1638, when the formal *recepta Unitaria Religio* was published.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The word *Unitarian* had been circulating in private letters in England, in reference to imported copies of such publications as the *[Library of the Polish Brethren who are called Unitarians](/wiki/Bibliotheca_Fratrum_Polonorum "Bibliotheca Fratrum Polonorum")* (1665). [Henry Hedworth](/wiki/Henry_Hedworth "Henry Hedworth") was the first to use the word "Unitarian" in print in English (1673), and the word first appears in a title in [Stephen Nye](/wiki/Stephen_Nye "Stephen Nye")'s *A Brief History of the Unitarians, called also Socinians* (1687). The movement gained popularity in England in the wake of the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") and began to become a formal denomination in 1774 when [Theophilus Lindsey](/wiki/Theophilus_Lindsey "Theophilus Lindsey") organised meetings with [Joseph Priestley](/wiki/Joseph_Priestley "Joseph Priestley"), founding the first avowedly Unitarian congregation in the country. This occurred at [Essex Street Church](/wiki/Essex_Street_Church "Essex Street Church") in London.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-16) Official [toleration came in 1813](/wiki/Doctrine_of_the_Trinity_Act_1813 "Doctrine of the Trinity Act 1813").

The first official acceptance of the Unitarian faith on the part of a congregation in America was by [King's Chapel](/wiki/King%27s_Chapel "King's Chapel") in Boston, which settled [James Freeman](/wiki/James_Freeman_(clergyman) "James Freeman (clergyman)") (1759–1835) in 1782, and revised the Prayer Book into a mild Unitarian liturgy in 1785. In 1800, [Joseph Stevens Buckminster](/wiki/Joseph_Stevens_Buckminster "Joseph Stevens Buckminster") became minister of the [Brattle Street Church](/wiki/Brattle_Street_Church "Brattle Street Church") in Boston, where his brilliant sermons, literary activities, and academic attention to the [German "New Criticism"](/wiki/Historical_criticism#Application "Historical criticism") helped shape the subsequent growth of Unitarianism in New England. Unitarian [Henry Ware](/wiki/Henry_Ware_(Unitarian) "Henry Ware (Unitarian)") (1764–1845) was appointed as the [Hollis professor of divinity](/wiki/Hollis_Chair_of_Divinity "Hollis Chair of Divinity") at Harvard College, in 1805. [Harvard Divinity School](/wiki/Harvard_Divinity_School "Harvard Divinity School") then shifted from its conservative roots to teach Unitarian theology (see [Harvard and Unitarianism](/wiki/Harvard_Divinity_School#Harvard_Divinity_School_and_Unitarianism "Harvard Divinity School")). Buckminster's close associate [William Ellery Channing](/wiki/William_Ellery_Channing "William Ellery Channing") (1780–1842) was settled over the [Federal Street Church](/wiki/Federal_Street_Church_(Boston) "Federal Street Church (Boston)") in Boston, 1803, and in a few years he became the leader of the Unitarian movement. A theological battle with the [Congregational Churches](/wiki/Congregationalism_in_the_United_States "Congregationalism in the United States") resulted in the formation of the [American Unitarian Association](/wiki/American_Unitarian_Association "American Unitarian Association") at Boston in 1825. Certainly, the unitarian theology was being "adopted" by the Congregationalists from the 1820s onwards. This movement is also evident in England at this time.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The first school founded by the Unitarians in the United States was the [Clinton Liberal Institute](/wiki/Clinton_Liberal_Institute "Clinton Liberal Institute"), in [Clinton, Oneida County, New York](/wiki/Clinton,_Oneida_County,_New_York "Clinton, Oneida County, New York"), founded in 1831.

## Beliefs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Beliefs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Egy_az_Isten_%21_-_2014.12.14.JPG/250px-Egy_az_Isten_%21_-_2014.12.14.JPG)](/wiki/File:Egy_az_Isten_!_-_2014.12.14.JPG)

"God is One" (*Egy az Isten*) stained glass window in a Unitarian church in [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), Hungary

### Christology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Christology")]

Unitarians charge that the doctrine of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") fails to adhere to strict monotheism. Unitarians maintain that Jesus was a great man and a [prophet](/wiki/Prophet "Prophet") of God—perhaps even a [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural") being—but not God himself.[[3]](#cite_note-Miano_2003_15-3) They believe Jesus did not claim to be God and that his teachings did not suggest the existence of a [triune God](/wiki/Triune_God "Triune God").

Unitarian [Christology](/wiki/Christology "Christology") can be divided according to whether or not Jesus is believed to have had a pre-human existence. Both forms maintain that [God](/wiki/God "God") is one [being](/wiki/Ousia "Ousia") and one [person](/wiki/Hypostasis_(philosophy_and_religion) "Hypostasis (philosophy and religion)") and that Jesus is the (or a) [Son of God](/wiki/Son_of_God "Son of God"), but generally not God himself.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In the early 19th century, Unitarian [Robert Wallace](/wiki/Robert_Wallace_(Unitarian) "Robert Wallace (Unitarian)") identified three particular classes of Unitarian doctrines in history:

* **[Arian](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism")**, which believed in a pre-existence of the [Logos](/wiki/Logos_(Christianity) "Logos (Christianity)");
* **[Socinian](/wiki/Socinianism "Socinianism")**, which denied his pre-existence but agreed that Jesus should be [worshipped](/wiki/Worship "Worship");
* **"Strict Unitarian"**, which, believing in an "incommunicable divinity of God", denied the worship of "the man Christ."[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Unitarianism is considered a factor in the decline of classical [deism](/wiki/Deism "Deism") because believers increasingly preferred to identify themselves as Unitarians rather than deists.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

#### "Socinian" Christology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: \"Socinian\" Christology")]

Main article: [Socinianism](/wiki/Socinianism "Socinianism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Portret_van_de_theoloog_Fausto_Paolo_Sozzini%2C_RP-P-1908-3942.jpg/250px-Portret_van_de_theoloog_Fausto_Paolo_Sozzini%2C_RP-P-1908-3942.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portret_van_de_theoloog_Fausto_Paolo_Sozzini,_RP-P-1908-3942.jpg)

[Fausto Sozzini](/wiki/Fausto_Sozzini "Fausto Sozzini") was an Italian theologian who helped define Unitarianism and also served the [Polish Brethren](/wiki/Polish_Brethren "Polish Brethren") church.

The Christology commonly called "[Socinian](/wiki/Socinianism "Socinianism")"—after [Fausto Sozzini](/wiki/Fausto_Sozzini "Fausto Sozzini"), one of the founders of Unitarian theology—refers to the belief that Jesus began his life when he was born as a human. In other words, the teaching that Jesus [pre-existed](/wiki/Pre-existence_of_Christ "Pre-existence of Christ") his human body is rejected. There are various views ranging from the belief that Jesus was simply a human ([psilanthropism](/wiki/Psilanthropism "Psilanthropism")) who, because of his greatness, was adopted by God as his Son ([adoptionism](/wiki/Adoptionism "Adoptionism")) to the belief that Jesus literally became the [son of God](/wiki/Son_of_God_(Christianity) "Son of God (Christianity)") when he was conceived by the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity").[[a]](#cite_note-41)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This Christology existed in some form or another before Sozzini. [Theodotus of Byzantium](/wiki/Theodotus_of_Byzantium "Theodotus of Byzantium"),[[41]](#cite_note-42) [Artemon](/wiki/Artemon "Artemon")[[42]](#cite_note-43) and [Paul of Samosata](/wiki/Paul_of_Samosata "Paul of Samosata")[[43]](#cite_note-44) denied the pre-existence of the [Christ](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)"). These ideas were continued by [Marcellus of Ancyra](/wiki/Marcellus_of_Ancyra "Marcellus of Ancyra") and his pupil [Photinus](/wiki/Photinus "Photinus") in the 4th century AD.[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46) In the [Radical Reformation](/wiki/Radical_Reformation "Radical Reformation") and [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") movements of the 16th century, this idea resurfaced with Sozzini's uncle, [Lelio Sozzini](/wiki/Lelio_Sozzini "Lelio Sozzini"). Having influenced the [Polish Brethren](/wiki/Polish_Brethren "Polish Brethren") to a formal declaration of this belief in the [Racovian Catechism](/wiki/Racovian_Catechism "Racovian Catechism"), Sozzini involuntarily ended up giving his name to this Christological position,[[46]](#cite_note-47) which continued with English Unitarians, such as [John Biddle](/wiki/John_Biddle_(Unitarian) "John Biddle (Unitarian)"), [Thomas Belsham](/wiki/Thomas_Belsham "Thomas Belsham"), [Theophilus Lindsey](/wiki/Theophilus_Lindsey "Theophilus Lindsey"), and [James Martineau](/wiki/James_Martineau "James Martineau"). In America, most of the early Unitarians were [Arian](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism") in Christology (see below), but among those who held to a "Socinian" view was [James Freeman](/wiki/James_Freeman_(clergyman) "James Freeman (clergyman)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Regarding the [virgin birth of Jesus](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus"), among those who denied the preexistence of the [Christ](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)"), some held to it, and others did not. Its denial is sometimes ascribed to the [Ebionites](/wiki/Ebionites "Ebionites"); however, [Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen") (*[Contra Celsum](/wiki/Contra_Celsum "Contra Celsum")* volume 61) and [Eusebius](/wiki/Eusebius "Eusebius") (*HE* iii.27) both indicate that some Ebionites did accept the virgin birth.[[47]](#cite_note-48) On the other hand, [Theodotus of Byzantium](/wiki/Theodotus_of_Byzantium "Theodotus of Byzantium"), [Artemon](/wiki/Artemon "Artemon"), and [Paul of Samosata](/wiki/Paul_of_Samosata "Paul of Samosata") all accepted the virgin birth.[[48]](#cite_note-Stead-49) In the early days of Unitarianism, the stories of the virgin birth were accepted by most. There were a number of Unitarians who questioned the [historicity of the Bible](/wiki/Historicity_of_the_Bible "Historicity of the Bible"), including [Symon Budny](/wiki/Symon_Budny "Symon Budny"), [Jacob Palaeologus](/wiki/Jacob_Palaeologus "Jacob Palaeologus"), [Thomas Belsham](/wiki/Thomas_Belsham "Thomas Belsham"), and [Richard Wright](/wiki/Richard_Wright_(Unitarian) "Richard Wright (Unitarian)"), and this made them question the virgin birth story.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53) Beginning in England and America in the 1830s and manifesting itself primarily in [transcendentalist Unitarianism](/wiki/Transcendentalist_Unitarianism "Transcendentalist Unitarianism"), which emerged from the German [liberal theology](/wiki/Liberal_theology "Liberal theology") associated primarily with [Friedrich Schleiermacher](/wiki/Friedrich_Schleiermacher "Friedrich Schleiermacher"), the psilanthropist view increased in popularity.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Its proponents took an intellectual and humanistic approach to religion. They embraced evolutionary concepts, asserted the "inherent goodness of man", and abandoned the doctrine of [biblical infallibility](/wiki/Biblical_infallibility "Biblical infallibility"), rejecting most of the miraculous events in the Christian Bible (including the virgin birth). Notable examples are [James Martineau](/wiki/James_Martineau "James Martineau"), [Theodore Parker](/wiki/Theodore_Parker "Theodore Parker"), [Ralph Waldo Emerson](/wiki/Ralph_Waldo_Emerson "Ralph Waldo Emerson") and [Frederic Henry Hedge](/wiki/Frederic_Henry_Hedge "Frederic Henry Hedge"). Famous American Unitarian [William Ellery Channing](/wiki/William_Ellery_Channing "William Ellery Channing") was a believer in the virgin birth until later in his life, after he had begun his association with the Transcendentalists.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57)

#### Arianism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Arianism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Constantine_burning_Arian_books_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Constantine_burning_Arian_books_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Constantine_burning_Arian_books_(cropped).jpg)

[Constantine I](/wiki/Constantine_I "Constantine I") burning [Arian](/wiki/Arian "Arian") books, illustration from a book of canon law, c. 825

[Arianism](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism") is often considered a form of Unitarianism.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

The Christology of Arianism holds that Jesus, before his human life, existed as the [Logos](/wiki/Logos_(Christianity) "Logos (Christianity)") (Word), a being begotten or created by God, who dwelt with God in [Heaven](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There are many varieties of this form of Unitarianism, ranging from the belief that the Son was a divine spirit of the same substance (called [subordinationism](/wiki/Subordinationism "Subordinationism")) or [of a similar substance](/wiki/Homoiousian "Homoiousian") to that of God (called [semi-Arianism](/wiki/Semi-Arianism "Semi-Arianism")) to the belief that he was an angel or other lesser spirit creature of a wholly different nature from God.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Not all of these views necessarily were held by [Arius](/wiki/Arius "Arius"), the namesake of Arianism. It is still nontrinitarian because, according to it, Jesus was and is a being always been beneath God in divinity, though higher than humans. Arian Christology was not the majority view among Unitarians in Poland, Transylvania, or England. It was only with the advent of American Unitarianism that it gained a foothold in the Unitarian movement.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Among early Christian theologians who believed in a pre-existent Jesus subordinate to God the Father were [Lucian of Antioch](/wiki/Lucian_of_Antioch "Lucian of Antioch"), [Eusebius of Caesarea](/wiki/Eusebius_of_Caesarea "Eusebius of Caesarea"), [Arius](/wiki/Arius "Arius"), [Eusebius of Nicomedia](/wiki/Eusebius_of_Nicomedia "Eusebius of Nicomedia"), [Asterius the Sophist](/wiki/Asterius_the_Sophist "Asterius the Sophist"), [Eunomius](/wiki/Eunomius "Eunomius"), and [Ulfilas](/wiki/Ulfilas "Ulfilas"), as well as [Felix, Bishop of Urgell](/wiki/Felix,_Bishop_of_Urgell "Felix, Bishop of Urgell"). Proponents also associate it (more controversially) with [Justin Martyr](/wiki/Justin_Martyr "Justin Martyr") and [Hippolytus of Rome](/wiki/Hippolytus_of_Rome "Hippolytus of Rome"). Antitrinitarian [Michael Servetus](/wiki/Michael_Servetus "Michael Servetus") did not deny the pre-existence of the Christ.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] In his *Treatise Concerning the Divine Trinity*, Servetus taught that the Logos was the reflection of Jesus, that said reflection of the Logos was the Word with God "and it was God Himself".[[59]](#cite_note-Servetus_1553_75-60) He further wrote that "the Word was the very essence of God or the manifestation of God's essence, and there was in God no other substance or hypostasis than His Word, in a bright cloud where God then seemed to subsist. And in that very spot the face and personality of Christ shone bright."[[59]](#cite_note-Servetus_1553_75-60) [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") held Arian beliefs as well.[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) Other 19th-century Arian Unitarians included [Andrews Norton](/wiki/Andrews_Norton "Andrews Norton")[[63]](#cite_note-64) and [William Ellery Channing](/wiki/William_Ellery_Channing "William Ellery Channing") (in his earlier years).[[64]](#cite_note-65)

### Other beliefs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Other beliefs")]

Although there is no specific authority on Unitarian beliefs aside from their inclusion of the rejection of the Trinity, the following beliefs are generally accepted:[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71)

* One God and the oneness or unity of God.
* The life and teachings of Jesus constitute the exemplary model for living one's own life.
* Reason, rational thought, science, and philosophy coexist with faith in God.
* Humans have the ability to exercise [free will](/wiki/Free_will "Free will") in a responsible, constructive and ethical manner with the assistance of religion.
* [Human nature](/wiki/Human_nature "Human nature") in its present condition is neither inherently corrupt nor depraved (see [original sin](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin")) but capable of both good and evil, as God intended.
* No religion can claim an absolute monopoly on the Holy Spirit or theological truth.
* Though God inspired the authors of the Christian Bible, they were humans and, therefore, subject to human error.
* The traditional [doctrines](/wiki/Doctrines "Doctrines") of [predestination](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination"), [Hell](/wiki/Hell_in_Christianity "Hell in Christianity"), and the [vicarious sacrifice](/wiki/Substitutionary_atonement "Substitutionary atonement") and [satisfaction](/wiki/Satisfaction_theory_of_atonement "Satisfaction theory of atonement") theories of the [atonement](/wiki/Atonement_in_Christianity "Atonement in Christianity") are invalid because they malign God's character and veil the true nature and mission of Jesus.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

In 1938, *The Christian Leader* attributed "*the* religion *of* Jesus, not *a* religion *about* Jesus", to Unitarians,[[72]](#cite_note-73) though the phrase was used earlier by [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist") [Rollin Lynde Hartt](/wiki/Rollin_Lynde_Hartt "Rollin Lynde Hartt") in 1924.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

## Worship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Worship")]

Worship within the Unitarian tradition accommodates a wide range of understandings of [God](/wiki/God "God"), while the focus of the service may be simply the celebration of life itself. Each Unitarian congregation is at liberty to devise its own form of worship, though commonly, Unitarians will light their chalice[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] (symbol of faith), have a story for all ages; and include sermons, prayers, hymns and songs. Some will allow attendees to publicly share their recent joys or concerns.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

## Modern Christian Unitarian organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Modern Christian Unitarian organizations")]

Main article: [History of Unitarianism](/wiki/History_of_Unitarianism "History of Unitarianism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/1st-Unitarian.jpg/250px-1st-Unitarian.jpg)](/wiki/File:1st-Unitarian.jpg)

[First Unitarian Meeting House](/wiki/First_Unitarian_Society_of_Madison "First Unitarian Society of Madison") in [Madison, Wisconsin](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin"), designed by Unitarian [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright")

This section relates to Unitarian churches and organizations today which are still specifically Christian, whether within or outside Unitarian Universalism. Unitarian Universalism, conversely, refers to the embracing of non-Christian religions.

### International groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: International groups")]

See also: [International Council of Unitarians and Universalists](/wiki/International_Council_of_Unitarians_and_Universalists "International Council of Unitarians and Universalists")

Some Unitarian Christian groups are affiliated with the [International Council of Unitarians and Universalists](/wiki/International_Council_of_Unitarians_and_Universalists "International Council of Unitarians and Universalists") (ICUU), founded in 1995.[[75]](#cite_note-76) The ICUU has "full member" groups in Australia, New Zealand, United Kingdom, Canada, Czech Republic, Denmark, EUU, Finland, Germany, Hungary, Indonesia, India, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines, Poland,[[76]](#cite_note-77) Romania, South Africa, Spain. Sri Lanka and the United States. Brazil is a Provisional Member.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

The ICUU includes small "Associate Groups", including Congregazione Italiana Cristiano Unitariana, [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin") (founded in 2004)[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80) and the Bét Dávid Unitarian Association, [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") (founded 2005).[[80]](#cite_note-81)

### Transylvania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Transylvania")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Derzs1.jpg/250px-Derzs1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Derzs1.jpg)

The [Dârjiu fortified church](/wiki/D%C3%A2rjiu_fortified_church "Dârjiu fortified church"), a 13th-century fortified church belonging to the [Unitarian Church of Transylvania](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_of_Transylvania "Unitarian Church of Transylvania"). This is the only Unitarian fortified church in Transylvania which is on the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List").

The largest Unitarian denomination worldwide today is also the oldest Unitarian denomination (since 1565, first use of the term "Unitarian" 1600):[[81]](#cite_note-82) the [Unitarian Church of Transylvania](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_of_Transylvania "Unitarian Church of Transylvania") (in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), which is in union with the Unitarian Church in Hungary). The church in Transylvania still looks to the statement of faith, the *[Summa Universae Theologiae Christianae secundum Unitarios](/wiki/Summa_Universae_Theologiae_Christianae_secundum_Unitarios "Summa Universae Theologiae Christianae secundum Unitarios")* (1787), though today assent to this is not required. The modern [Unitarian Church in Hungary](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_in_Hungary "Unitarian Church in Hungary") (25,000 members) and the [Transylvanian Unitarian Church](/wiki/Transylvanian_Unitarian_Church "Transylvanian Unitarian Church") (75,000 members) are affiliated with the International Council of Unitarians and Universalists (ICUU) and claim continuity with the historical Unitarian Christian tradition established by [Ferenc Dávid](/wiki/Ferenc_D%C3%A1vid "Ferenc Dávid") in 1565 in [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") under [John II Sigismund Zápolya](/wiki/John_II_Sigismund_Z%C3%A1polya "John II Sigismund Zápolya"). The Unitarian churches in Hungary and Transylvania are structured and organized along a church hierarchy that includes the election by the synod of a national bishop who serves as superintendent of the Church. Many Hungarian Unitarians embrace the principles of rationalist Unitarianism.[[82]](#cite_note-83) Unitarian high schools exist only in Transylvania (Romania), including the [John Sigismund Unitarian Academy](/wiki/John_Sigismund_Unitarian_Academy "John Sigismund Unitarian Academy") in [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca"), the [Protestant Theological Institute of Cluj](/wiki/Protestant_Theological_Institute_of_Cluj "Protestant Theological Institute of Cluj"), and the Berde Mózes Unitárius Gimnázium in [Cristuru Secuiesc](/wiki/Cristuru_Secuiesc "Cristuru Secuiesc"); both teach Rationalist Unitarianism.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Unitarian_chapel_newington_green.jpg/250px-Unitarian_chapel_newington_green.jpg)](/wiki/File:Unitarian_chapel_newington_green.jpg)

[Newington Green Unitarian Church](/wiki/Newington_Green_Unitarian_Church "Newington Green Unitarian Church") in London, England. Built in 1708, this is the oldest [nonconformist](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") church in London still in use.

The [Unitarian Christian Association](/wiki/Unitarian_Christian_Association "Unitarian Christian Association") (UCA) was founded in the United Kingdom in 1991 by Rev. [Lancelot Garrard](/w/index.php?title=Lancelot_Garrard&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lancelot Garrard (page does not exist)") (1904–1993)[[83]](#cite_note-84) and others to promote specifically Christian ideas within the [General Assembly of Unitarian and Free Christian Churches](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_Unitarian_and_Free_Christian_Churches "General Assembly of Unitarian and Free Christian Churches") (GAUFCC), the national Unitarian body in the UK. Just as the UUCF and ICUU maintain formal links with the [Unitarian Universalist Association](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalist_Association "Unitarian Universalist Association") in the US, so the UCA is an affiliate body of the GAUFCC in the UK.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The majority of Unitarian Christian publications are sponsored by an organization and published specifically for their membership. Generally, they do not serve as a tool for missionary work or encouraging conversions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: India")]

In India, three different schools of Unitarian thought influenced varying movements, including the [Brahmo Samaj](/wiki/Brahmo_Samaj "Brahmo Samaj"), the Unitarian Church of the [Khasi Hills](/wiki/Khasi_Hills "Khasi Hills"),[[84]](#cite_note-85) and the Unitarian Christian Church of [Chennai](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai"), in Madras, founded in 1795.[[85]](#cite_note-86) As of 2011, "Thirty-five congregations and eight fellowships comprising almost 10,000 Unitarians now form the Unitarian Union of North East India."[[86]](#cite_note-87)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: United States")]

The American Unitarian Conference (AUC) was formed in 2000 and stands between UUA and ICUU in attachment to the Christian element of modern Unitarianism. The American Unitarian Conference is open to non-Christian Unitarians, being particularly popular with non-Christian [theists](/wiki/Theists "Theists") and [deists](/wiki/Deists "Deists").[[87]](#cite_note-88) As of 2009, the AUC has three congregations in the United States.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

Unitarian Christian Ministries International was a Unitarian ministry incorporated in South Carolina until its dissolution in 2013 when it merged with the Unitarian Christian Emerging Church. The Unitarian Christian Emerging Church has recently undergone reorganization and today is known as the Unitarian Christian Church of America. In addition, the Unitarian Universalist Faith Alliance and Ministries follow a Progressive Christian format honoring Sacred Space and Creation Spirituality.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

The Unitarian Christian Church of America (UCCA) was formed on 1 October 2016 through the merging of the Unitarian Christian Emerging Church and the Unitarian Christian Conference. The church's current ministry in on-line and through local fellowship gatherings. The current senior pastor and current president of the UCCA is the Reverend Dr. Shannon Rogers. The UCCA has both ordained and lay members.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

### Australia and New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Australia and New Zealand")]

Further information: [Australia and New Zealand Unitarian Universalist Association](/wiki/Australia_and_New_Zealand_Unitarian_Universalist_Association "Australia and New Zealand Unitarian Universalist Association")

The first Unitarian Church in Australia was built in 1854 in Melbourne and was followed soon afterwards by chapels in Sydney and Adelaide, and later regional centres including Ballarat.[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93) The modern church, no longer unitarian Christian, retains properties in Adelaide, Sydney and Melbourne, and smaller congregations elsewhere in Australia and New Zealand.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: South Africa")]

The Unitarian movement in South Africa was founded in 1867 by [David Faure](/wiki/David_Faure "David Faure"),[[94]](#cite_note-95) member of a well-known Cape family. He encountered advanced liberal religious thought while completing his studies at the [University of Leiden](/wiki/University_of_Leiden "University of Leiden") in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") for the ministry of the [Dutch Reformed Church](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church_in_South_Africa "Dutch Reformed Church in South Africa") in [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town").

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ireland")]

There are two active [Unitarian churches](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_in_Ireland "Unitarian Church in Ireland") in Ireland, one in Dublin and the other in Cork. Both are member churches of the [Non-subscribing Presbyterian Church of Ireland](/wiki/Non-subscribing_Presbyterian_Church_of_Ireland "Non-subscribing Presbyterian Church of Ireland").

### Denmark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Denmark")]

Unitarianism was a latecomer to Denmark. Some of the inspiration came from Norway and England – family members of the founders, and the wife of Edward Grieg. 1900–1918 the society priest was Uffe Birkedal, who had previously been a Lutheran priest. He held the first worship 18 February 1900. A founding general assembly 18 May 1900 elected Mary Bess Westenholz as the first chairman of the Society. The Society newsletter was named *Protestantisk Tidende* 1904–1993, and then renamed *Unitaren*, reflecting a gradually changing perception of being part of the Danish Lutheran Church, to one where this was no longer assumed ([[95]](#cite_note-96)).

### Biblical Unitarians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Biblical Unitarians")]

Main article: [Biblical Unitarianism](/wiki/Biblical_Unitarianism "Biblical Unitarianism")

[Biblical Unitarianism](/wiki/Biblical_Unitarianism "Biblical Unitarianism") identifies the [Christian belief](/wiki/Christianity#Beliefs "Christianity") that the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") teaches that God the Father is [one singular being](/wiki/Monotheism "Monotheism"), and that [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") is a distinct being, his son, but not divine.[[96]](#cite_note-Tuggy_2020-97) [A few denominations](/wiki/Biblical_Unitarianism#Denominations "Biblical Unitarianism") use this term to describe themselves, clarifying the distinction between them and those churches which, from the late 19th century, evolved into [modern British Unitarianism](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_Unitarian_and_Free_Christian_Churches "General Assembly of Unitarian and Free Christian Churches") and, primarily in the United States, [Unitarian Universalism](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism").[[96]](#cite_note-Tuggy_2020-97) In 16th-century Italy, Biblical Unitarianism was powered by the ideas of the Non-trinitarian theologians [Lelio](/wiki/Lelio_Sozzini "Lelio Sozzini") and [Fausto Sozzini](/wiki/Fausto_Sozzini "Fausto Sozzini"), founders of [Socinianism](/wiki/Socinianism "Socinianism");[[97]](#cite_note-Mortimer_2010-98) their doctrine was embraced and further developed by the [Unitarian Church of Transylvania](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_of_Transylvania "Unitarian Church of Transylvania") during the 16th and 17th centuries.[[98]](#cite_note-99) Today, it's represented by the churches associated with the [Christian Church in Italy](/w/index.php?title=Christian_Church_in_Italy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Christian Church in Italy (page does not exist)").[[99]](#cite_note-100)

## Notable Unitarians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitarianism&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Notable Unitarians")]

Main article: [List of Unitarians, Universalists, and Unitarian Universalists](/wiki/List_of_Unitarians,_Universalists,_and_Unitarian_Universalists "List of Unitarians, Universalists, and Unitarian Universalists")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/GodfreyKneller-IsaacNewton-1689.jpg/250px-GodfreyKneller-IsaacNewton-1689.jpg)](/wiki/File:GodfreyKneller-IsaacNewton-1689.jpg)

[Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") held [Arian](/wiki/Arian "Arian") views.

Notable Unitarians include classical composers [Edvard Grieg](/wiki/Edvard_Grieg "Edvard Grieg") and [Béla Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók"); [Ralph Waldo Emerson](/wiki/Ralph_Waldo_Emerson "Ralph Waldo Emerson"), [Theodore Parker](/wiki/Theodore_Parker "Theodore Parker"), [Yveon Seon](/wiki/Yvonne_Seon "Yvonne Seon") and [Thomas Lamb Eliot](/wiki/Thomas_Lamb_Eliot "Thomas Lamb Eliot") in theology and ministry; [Oliver Heaviside](/wiki/Oliver_Heaviside "Oliver Heaviside"), [Erasmus Darwin](/wiki/Erasmus_Darwin "Erasmus Darwin"), [Joseph Priestley](/wiki/Joseph_Priestley "Joseph Priestley"), [John Archibald Wheeler](/wiki/John_Archibald_Wheeler "John Archibald Wheeler"), [Linus Pauling](/wiki/Linus_Pauling "Linus Pauling"), [Sir Isaac Newton](/wiki/Sir_Isaac_Newton "Sir Isaac Newton")[[100]](#cite_note-101) and inventor Sir [Francis Ronalds](/wiki/Francis_Ronalds "Francis Ronalds")[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103) in science; [George Boole](/wiki/George_Boole "George Boole") in mathematics; [Susan B. Anthony](/wiki/Susan_B._Anthony "Susan B. Anthony") in civil government; [Frances Ellen Watkins Harper](/wiki/Frances_Harper "Frances Harper"), [Whitney Young](/wiki/Whitney_Young "Whitney Young") of the [National Urban League](/wiki/National_Urban_League "National Urban League"), and [Florence Nightingale](/wiki/Florence_Nightingale "Florence Nightingale") in humanitarianism and social justice; [John Bowring](/wiki/John_Bowring "John Bowring"), [Samuel Taylor Coleridge](/wiki/Samuel_Taylor_Coleridge "Samuel Taylor Coleridge") and [Elizabeth Gaskell](/wiki/Elizabeth_Gaskell "Elizabeth Gaskell") in literature; [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright") in the arts; [Josiah Wedgwood](/wiki/Josiah_Wedgwood "Josiah Wedgwood"), [Richard Peacock](/wiki/Richard_Peacock "Richard Peacock")[[103]](#cite_note-104) and [Samuel Carter](/wiki/Samuel_Carter_(Coventry_MP) "Samuel Carter (Coventry MP)") MP[[104]](#cite_note-105) in industry; [Thomas Starr King](/wiki/Thomas_Starr_King "Thomas Starr King") in ministry and politics; and [Charles William Eliot](/wiki/Charles_William_Eliot "Charles William Eliot") in education. [Julia Ward Howe](/wiki/Julia_Ward_Howe "Julia Ward Howe") was a leader in the woman suffrage movement, the first ever woman to be elected to the Academy of Arts and Letters, and author of the "[Battle Hymn of the Republic](/wiki/Battle_Hymn_of_the_Republic "Battle Hymn of the Republic")", volumes of poetry, and other writing. Although raised a Quaker, [Ezra Cornell](/wiki/Ezra_Cornell "Ezra Cornell"), founder of [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University") in Ithaca, New York, attended the Unitarian church and was one of the founders of Ithaca's First Unitarian Church. Eramus Darwin Shattuck, a signatory to the Oregon State Constitution, founded the first Unitarian church in Oregon in 1865.[[105]](#cite_note-106)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Frances_Ellen_Watkins_Harper%2C_1825-1911_LCCN_2002698208.jpg/250px-Frances_Ellen_Watkins_Harper%2C_1825-1911_LCCN_2002698208.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frances_Ellen_Watkins_Harper,_1825-1911_LCCN_2002698208.jpg)

[Frances Ellen Watkins Harper](/wiki/Frances_Ellen_Watkins_Harper "Frances Ellen Watkins Harper") was an abolitionist, journalist, and suffragist associated with both American Unitarianism and the [African Methodist Episcopal Church](/wiki/African_Methodist_Episcopal_Church "African Methodist Episcopal Church").

Eleven Nobel Prizes have been awarded to Unitarians: [Robert Millikan](/wiki/Robert_Millikan "Robert Millikan")[[106]](#cite_note-107) and [John Bardeen](/wiki/John_Bardeen "John Bardeen") (twice) in physics; [Emily Green Balch](/wiki/Emily_Green_Balch "Emily Green Balch"), [Albert Schweitzer](/wiki/Albert_Schweitzer "Albert Schweitzer") and [Linus Pauling](/wiki/Linus_Pauling "Linus Pauling") for peace; [George Wald](/wiki/George_Wald "George Wald") and [David H. Hubel](/wiki/David_H._Hubel "David H. Hubel") in medicine; [Linus Pauling](/wiki/Linus_Pauling "Linus Pauling") in chemistry; and [Herbert A. Simon](/wiki/Herbert_A._Simon "Herbert A. Simon") in economics.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Four presidents of the United States were Unitarians: [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams"), [John Quincy Adams](/wiki/John_Quincy_Adams "John Quincy Adams"), [Millard Fillmore](/wiki/Millard_Fillmore "Millard Fillmore"), and [William Howard Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft "William Howard Taft").[[107]](#cite_note-108) [Adlai Stevenson II](/wiki/Adlai_Stevenson_II "Adlai Stevenson II"), the Democratic presidential nominee in 1952 and 1956, was a Unitarian; he was the last Unitarian to be nominated by a major party for president as of 2024.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Although a self-styled materialist, [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") was pro-Unitarian to the extent of suggesting that it would become the predominant religion in the United States.[[108]](#cite_note-109) John Adams is widely considered the first Unitarian president, while some describe him as Christian Deist.[[109]](#cite_note-Oxford-Adams-110) In a 1813 letter to Jefferson, Adams asserted that the Christian Trinity was a "fabrication" derived from Pythagorean and Platonic philosophies rather than divine revelation, and expressed surprise that theologian [Joseph Priestley](/wiki/Joseph_Priestley "Joseph Priestley") had overlooked these connections.[[110]](#cite_note-Adams-Dec-1813-Tri-111)

In the United Kingdom, although Unitarianism was the religion of only a small minority of the population, its practitioners had an enormous impact on Victorian politics, not only in the larger cities – [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") – but in smaller communities such as [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester "Leicester"), where there were so many Unitarian mayors that the Unitarian Chapel was known as the "Mayors' Nest".[[111]](#cite_note-Dare-112) Numerous Unitarian families were highly significant in the social and political life of Britain from Victorian times to the middle of the 20th century. They included the [Nettlefolds](/wiki/Joseph_Nettlefold "Joseph Nettlefold"), [Martineaus](/wiki/Martineau_family "Martineau family"), [Luptons](/wiki/Lupton_family "Lupton family"), [Kitsons](/wiki/James_Kitson,_1st_Baron_Airedale "James Kitson, 1st Baron Airedale"), [Chamberlains](/wiki/Joseph_Chamberlain "Joseph Chamberlain") and Kenricks.[[112]](#cite_note-113)[[113]](#cite_note-114) In [Birmingham, England](/wiki/Birmingham,_England "Birmingham, England"), a Unitarian church – the Church of the Messiah – was opened in 1862. It became a cultural and intellectual centre of a whole society, a place where ideas about society were openly and critically discussed.[[b]](#cite_note-117)

In Luke, the angel Gabriel tells Mary she will conceive through the Holy Spirit, and in Matthew, the angel explains to Joseph that the child conceived in Mary is of the Holy Spirit.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-117)**  Henry W. Crosskey's congregation included [Joseph Chamberlain](/wiki/Joseph_Chamberlain "Joseph Chamberlain"), father of British prime minister [Neville Chamberlain](/wiki/Neville_Chamberlain "Neville Chamberlain")[[111]](#cite_note-Dare-112) and Arthur, his younger brother, who was married to Louisa Kenrick; William Kenrick, his brother-in-law, who was married to Mary Chamberlain; and [Sir Thomas Martineau](/wiki/Martineau_family "Martineau family"), who was the nephew of [Harriet Martineau](/wiki/Harriet_Martineau "Harriet Martineau"), another outspoken public figure and author. Sir Thomas Martineau (died 1893) was related to the [Chamberlain family](/wiki/Neville_Chamberlain "Neville Chamberlain") by marriage; Sir Thomas had married Emily Kenrick, the sister of Florence Chamberlain, née Kenrick.[[114]](#cite_note-115) In Lambeth, South London, another two members of the Martineau family, Caroline and Constance, worked at Morley College, the former acting as (unpaid) principal for over 11 years. Several other prominent Unitarians were involved in the development of this liberal arts college, which was founded by actors at the Old Vic theatre.[[115]](#cite_note-116)

21. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** Letter from Matthew F. Smith to Editor *World faiths Encounter*, 7–12 World Congress of Faiths – 1994 – "In an otherwise excellent article by Jasbir Singh Ahluwalia, 'Sikh Spirit in an Age of Plurality' (No. 6, November 1993), the writer makes a number of pejorative remarks about 'unitarianism', associating the term with a striving for a monolithic polity and reductionism to a common denominator. This is a very unfortunate misuse of the word. A correct definition of 'unitarianism' (small 'u') is the mono-[hypo-static](/wiki/Hypostasis_(philosophy)#Trinitarian_definitions "Hypostasis (philosophy)") belief system of someone not directly associated with the Unitarian movement, almost always applied to a person from the Christian tradition, as the word was coined in distinction to the orthodox 'Trinitarian' doctrine of Christianity. 'Unitarians' (capital 'U') are, of course, those who follow the Unitarian approach to religion and are formally associated with the movement. In neither case can it be claimed that there is an underlying agenda towards reductionism and uniformity. Quite the reverse, in fact. Modern Unitarianism is remarkable among religions in not only welcoming the variety of faiths that there are to be found but also, as a creedless church, welcoming and encouraging acceptance of the same. We readily accept that not all our members are 'realist' theists, for example. Our long-standing commitment to interfaith understanding, evident in our practical support of the International Association for Religious Freedom, the World Congress of Faiths and the newly established International Interfaith centre in Oxford cannot be taken to mean that Unitarians are seeking the creation of a single world religion out of the old. I do not know a single Unitarian who believes or seeks that. On the contrary, we reject uniformity and cherish instead the highest degree of spiritual integrity, both of the existing religious traditions of the world and of religious persons as unique, thinking individuals. Matthew F Smith, Information Officer" ([Essex Street Chapel](/wiki/Essex_Street_Chapel "Essex Street Chapel"), Unitarian Church headquarters, UK)

[Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20170311130654/https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/trinity/unitarianism.html) from the original on 11 March 2017. Retrieved 9 August 2021. "There are presently a number of small Christian groups calling themselves "biblical unitarians" (or: Christian monotheists or one God believers) to distinguish themselves from late 19th to 21st century Unitarians and Unitarian Universalists. Their arguments draw on early modern unitarian sources, while eschewing some of the idiosyncrasies of [Socinus's theology](/wiki/Socinianism "Socinianism") and most of the extra revisions of the [Priestley](/wiki/Joseph_Priestley "Joseph Priestley")-derived stream of unitarians. Like late 18th to early 19th century unitarians, they argue at length that trinitarianism has no biblical foundation, and is inconsistent with its clear teachings. They also reject trinitarianism as contradictory or unintelligible, as involving [idolatry](/wiki/Idolatry_in_the_Bible "Idolatry in the Bible"), and as having been, as it were, illegally imported from [Platonic philosophy](/wiki/Platonic_philosophy "Platonic philosophy") [...]. On some issues they draw support from recent [biblical scholarship](/wiki/Biblical_studies "Biblical studies"), for example, the point that talk of "generation" and "procession" in the [Gospel of John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John") doesn't support later claims about inter-trinitarian relations [...]. Although this literature points out real tensions within contemporary theology (between text-oriented commentators and systematic theologians) it is widely ignored in academic theology and philosophy, and its adherents are generally excluded from the institutions of [mainstream Christianity](/wiki/Mainstream_Christianity "Mainstream Christianity")."
